topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle paper felt alien beneath my fingertips, yellowed and fragile, smelling faintly of dust and old ink. It was a letter, undeniably my father's, dated from the early nineteen-seventies. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary sound in the quiet study. As I unfolded the stiff sheet, the script swam before my eyes—formal, utterly detached, and laced with a tone of weary resignation that made my stomach clench. I reread the sentences, the precise, almost clinical language describing his departure, and a cold, hard knot tightened in my chest. I slowly crumpled the paper, the sound a faint, dry rustle, and shoved it deep into a drawer, wanting nothing more than to erase the evidence of that distant, hollow farewell.

[story 2]

I stared at the typed document under the harsh glare of the desk lamp. Decades had passed since I’d seen this—a formal notification of stepping down from a position he’d held for nearly twenty years. The words, meticulously crafted to sound professional and unburdened, struck me with a physical jolt. A wave of profound distaste washed over me, leaving a sour taste in my mouth. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning in protest, and rubbed my temples as if to physically scrub the memory of the letter away. Why did he write it like that? It felt like reading a document detailing a betrayal, a slow erosion of commitment, and I couldn't bring myself to look at it again.

[story 3]

My hands trembled as I held the envelope. It was heavy, weighted by the passage of time and the sheer weight of what it represented. When I finally broke the seal, the paper seemed to radiate a stale atmosphere. The handwriting was neat, almost painfully so, detailing the administrative exit from his career. A deep, unpleasant churning started low in my abdomen. I dropped the letter onto the floor, the sound echoing unnaturally loud in the room. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the rug, my jaw tight, unable to reconcile the image of the man who wrote those words with the emptiness I felt now. It was a cheap, flimsy justification for leaving, and the sight of it made me want to physically recoil.
